summary: Graham argues that the most damaging lesson from school is learning to optimize for grades by hacking tests rather than actually learning. Because authority-imposed tests are usually hackable, students are trained to chase proxies and carry that mindset into startups and work, where the real way to win is often simply doing good work. He suggests that recognizing and unlearning this habit frees individuals and society, especially as work increasingly rewards genuine outcomes over authority-approved signals.
key_points: Grades and tests often reward test-hacking over real learning, so students optimize for proxies.
Authority-imposed tests are usually hackable unless explicitly designed to be unhackable.
This mindset carries into startups, where founders overcomplicate and chase signals instead of building great products.
In many modern fields, especially startups and writing, you win by doing good work, not by hacking tests.
Unlearning the habit of test-hacking can redirect ambition toward work with real, unhackable outcomes.
money_quotes: The most damaging thing you learned in school wasn't something you learned in any specific class. It was learning to get good grades.
Getting a good grade in a class on x is so different from learning a lot about x that you have to choose one or the other, and you can't blame students if they choose grades.
The worst thing it does is to train you that the way to win is by hacking bad tests.
In fact, one of the most striking things about startups is the degree to which you win by simply doing good work.
Imagine what the world could look like if that happened.
key_insight: Schools train people to hack proxy tests, but real success—especially in startups—comes from doing genuinely good work, so the key is to unlearn the test-hacking mindset.
